title: JIT GC safepoints and heap allocation helpers
purpose: JIT-compiled code that allocates on the heap must cooperate with the per-process garbage collector. GC safepoints are locations in compiled code where the collector can safely pause execution, enumerate all live GC roots, and potentially relocate heap objects. Without safepoints, GC cannot collect during compiled execution — the process heap grows unbounded. Per D4 in the JIT/AOT design doc: safepoints at every allocation site, function call site, and yield point, with stack maps recording which registers and stack slots hold live Term values.
task: Implement GC safepoint emission in crates/beamr/src/jit/safepoint.rs and runtime allocation helpers in crates/beamr/src/jit/runtime.rs. Safepoint metadata is collected during IR construction and emitted as StackMapEntry values (from B-130 types.rs). Runtime helpers are extern 'C' functions callable from compiled code that allocate on the process heap and trigger GC when necessary. This brief does NOT wire safepoints into the interpreter dispatch loop (B-135) or into specific opcode translations (B-136 uses the helpers).

requirements.title: SafepointEmitter for Cranelift IR construction
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a SafepointEmitter that tracks live root locations during IR construction. WHEN a safepoint is needed (allocation site, call site, yield point), THE SYSTEM SHALL call SafepointEmitter::emit_safepoint(builder, instruction_offset) which records the current set of live roots as a StackMapEntry. THE SYSTEM SHALL track roots in two categories: Register(u16) for values in Cranelift SSA variables, and StackSlot(i32) for values spilled to the stack frame.
acceptance: SafepointEmitter::new() creates an emitter with no tracked roots
SafepointEmitter::track_root(location: RootLocation) adds a live root
SafepointEmitter::untrack_root(location: RootLocation) removes a live root
SafepointEmitter::emit_safepoint(offset: u32) -> StackMapEntry snapshots the current live roots
StackMapEntry::live_roots contains exactly the roots tracked at emission time
Test: track 3 roots, untrack 1, emit safepoint -> entry has 2 live roots
Test: emit two safepoints with different live root sets -> each entry captures its own snapshot

requirements.title: Safepoint emission at allocation sites
requirements.spec: WHEN the IR translator encounters a heap-allocating operation (put_tuple2, put_list, allocate, test_heap), THE SYSTEM SHALL emit a GC safepoint immediately before the allocation call. THE SYSTEM SHALL ensure the stack map at that point includes all Term values that are live across the allocation — any Term that will be used after the allocation must be in the root set. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT emit redundant safepoints for consecutive allocations within the same basic block if the root set has not changed.
acceptance: emit_allocation_safepoint(builder, emitter, offset) emits a safepoint entry with all live Term roots
The safepoint is emitted BEFORE the allocation call (so GC can run before allocating)
Consecutive allocations with identical root sets share a single safepoint entry
Consecutive allocations with different root sets emit separate safepoint entries
Test: IR with two consecutive put_list (same live set) -> 1 safepoint entry
Test: IR with put_list, move, put_tuple (different live set) -> 2 safepoint entries

requirements.title: Safepoint emission at call and yield sites
requirements.spec: WHEN the IR translator encounters a function call instruction (call, call_ext, call_only, call_last) or a yield point (reduction counter check), THE SYSTEM SHALL emit a GC safepoint. The callee may trigger GC (allocations in the callee, or the callee yields triggering GC). THE SYSTEM SHALL include in the root set all caller-saved Term values that are live after the call returns.
acceptance: emit_call_safepoint(builder, emitter, offset) emits a safepoint with caller-live Term roots
emit_yield_safepoint(builder, emitter, offset) emits a safepoint at yield points
The safepoint root set does NOT include values that are dead after the call (not used post-call)
Test: call with 2 live-after values and 1 dead-after value -> safepoint has 2 roots

requirements.title: Runtime helper — jit_alloc_tuple
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide an extern 'C' fn jit_alloc_tuple(process: *mut Process, arity: u32) -> u64 runtime helper that allocates a tuple of the given arity on the process heap and returns the tagged Term value. IF the process heap does not have enough space, THE SYSTEM SHALL trigger per-process GC (using the safepoint root set from the caller's stack map), then retry the allocation. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT call jit_alloc_tuple with arity 0 — zero-arity tuples are not valid in BEAM.
acceptance: jit_alloc_tuple(process, arity) allocates arity+1 words (header + elements) on the process heap
The returned u64 is a tagged Term::boxed pointer to the tuple header
The tuple header contains the correct arity in the BoxedHeader format
If heap space is insufficient: GC runs, heap grows, allocation succeeds
Test: allocate tuple of arity 3 -> returned Term is a valid boxed tuple with arity 3
Test: allocate when heap is nearly full -> GC triggers, allocation succeeds

requirements.title: Runtime helper — jit_alloc_cons
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide an extern 'C' fn jit_alloc_cons(process: *mut Process, head: u64, tail: u64) -> u64 runtime helper that allocates a cons cell on the process heap with the given head and tail, and returns the tagged list Term. IF the process heap does not have enough space, THE SYSTEM SHALL trigger per-process GC, then retry the allocation.
acceptance: jit_alloc_cons(process, head, tail) allocates 2 words (head + tail) on the process heap
The returned u64 is a tagged Term::list pointer to the cons cell
The cons cell head and tail match the input values
If heap space is insufficient: GC runs, heap grows, allocation succeeds
Test: allocate cons cell [1 | []] -> returned Term is a valid list
Test: allocate chain of 3 cons cells -> valid 3-element list

requirements.title: Runtime helper function pointers for Cranelift call instructions
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL expose jit_alloc_tuple and jit_alloc_cons as function pointers that Cranelift can reference in emitted call instructions. THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a RuntimeHelpers struct that holds the function pointer addresses, resolvable by JitCompiler during IR construction. THE SYSTEM SHALL use Cranelift's import_function mechanism to reference the helpers by their known addresses.
acceptance: RuntimeHelpers::new() returns a struct with jit_alloc_tuple_ptr and jit_alloc_cons_ptr fields
Each pointer is the address of the corresponding extern 'C' function
JitCompiler can declare these as imported functions in the Cranelift module
Cranelift emits direct call instructions to these addresses
Test: RuntimeHelpers::new().jit_alloc_tuple_ptr is non-null
Test: RuntimeHelpers function pointers are stable across multiple RuntimeHelpers::new() calls

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement on-stack replacement (OSR) — compiled code runs from function entry only
SHALL NOT implement escape analysis — all boxed terms go to the process heap
SHALL NOT implement stack walking for safepoints at this stage — safepoints record root locations, actual GC root enumeration during collection is the GC's responsibility
SHALL NOT implement tuple/list element writes in this brief — that is B-136's ir_terms.rs using these allocation helpers
SHALL NOT implement moving GC cooperation — the existing per-process GC handles relocation; this brief ensures roots are visible
verification: cargo test -p beamr passes with all tests green
safepoint.rs is under 500 lines of production code
runtime.rs is under 500 lines of production code
All runtime helpers are extern 'C' (FFI-safe for Cranelift calls)
No unsafe code in safepoint.rs — all unsafe is confined to runtime.rs where raw process pointers are dereferenced
